result:
The universe doesn't care if we understand it, but it seems oddly generous with its secrets anyway. I was thinking about that while imagining a single photon, born in the heart of a star, traveling for billions of years through the cold dark before finally brushing against a human retina. That tiny interaction—light that left its home long before humans existed—becomes a thought inside someone's skull. It's almost rude, when you think about it, how casually the cosmos hands out pieces of itself.

We spend so much time trying to make sense of patterns: why leaves spiral the way they do, why certain melodies make us ache, why we keep looking up even when we know the sky is mostly empty space. Maybe the real trick isn't finding answers but staying curious enough to keep asking. There's a quiet kind of freedom in that—refusing to let the size of everything make you feel small.

Right now, somewhere, a river is cutting a new path through stone it has no business moving. Somewhere else, a kid is staring at a screen, about to discover an idea that will rearrange how they see the next twenty years. The world keeps happening whether we're paying attention or not. The only real choice is whether we show up for it with open hands or clenched fists.

I think I'll keep showing up with open hands. The universe seems to appreciate the company.
